title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
text:
Chronic heart failure (CHF) is the terminal common pathological manifestation of various cardiovascular diseases and the leading cause of global cardiovascular mortality. The global prevalence of adult CHF continues to rise in recent years. Affected by genetic variation, environmental incentives and metabolic abnormalities, the incidence of refractory heart failure in young and middle-aged populations has increased year by year. Genetic cardiomyopathy is the primary cause of heart failure, malignant arrhythmia and sudden cardiac death in young and middle-aged individuals. Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM), the most common hereditary primary cardiomyopathy, is pathologically characterized by progressive biventricular dilation, ventricular wall thinning, and persistent irreversible decline in myocardial systolic function. Its pathological process involves cascading injuries including cardiomyocyte apoptosis, interstitial fibrosis, immune inflammatory infiltration, and energy metabolism exhaustion. Epidemiological data show that the 5-year survival rate of DCM patients is less than 60%, and more than 70% of end-stage DCM patients progress to refractory CHF, which is a key and difficult point in clinical cardiovascular intervention.
At present, standardized comprehensive intervention systems for DCM-CHF have been established clinically, mainly including neuroendocrine inhibitor drugs such as β-blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors/angiotensin receptor blockers, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists, as well as invasive treatments such as ventricular resynchronization device-assisted circulation and end-stage heart transplantation. These interventions can effectively antagonize excessive neuroendocrine activation, improve short-term hemodynamic indicators, relieve clinical symptoms such as chest tightness and fatigue, and reduce the risk of acute malignant cardiovascular events. However, single chemical drugs only target individual pathological targets, failing to cover the complex multi-dimensional pathological network of DCM-CHF, and cannot block core pathological links such as genetic variation-driven disease progression, persistent myocardial metabolic disorder, chronic low-grade inflammatory infiltration, and progressive ventricular remodeling. In addition, long-term Western drug intervention is accompanied by side effects such as drug resistance, hepatorenal damage and arrhythmia, while device therapy and heart transplantation have clinical limitations including high cost, donor shortage and postoperative rejection. These factors result in limited long-term prognosis improvement of DCM-CHF, and there is an urgent clinical need for safe and efficient multi-target synergistic adjuvant intervention strategies.

Different from the single-target regulatory mode of chemical drugs, medicinal plants are rich in structurally diverse and functionally specific natural small molecular compounds, which can synergistically regulate complex disease pathological networks through multi-component, multi-target and multi-pathway patterns, with the advantages of high safety, low side effects and stable long-term intervention efficacy. They have become core resources for adjuvant treatment of chronic complex diseases and development of novel lead compounds. In recent years, numerous in vivo and in vitro pharmacological experiments and clinical adjuvant studies have confirmed that active constituents of medicinal plants can improve ventricular remodeling and delay heart failure progression by regulating myocardial energy metabolism, inhibiting oxidative stress injury, antagonizing myocardial fibrosis, balancing cardiac immune microenvironment, and stabilizing calcium signal homeostasis, showing unique advantages in long-term adjuvant intervention of DCM-CHF.
Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus are the most classic and widely applied medicinal plant combination in the field of cardiovascular protection, with definite clinical efficacy in adjuvant treatment of heart failure. Modern pharmacological studies have verified that diterpenoid alkaloids from Aconitum carmichaelii possess significant cardiotonic, positive inotropic, calcium channel regulatory and anti-inflammatory activities, which can directly act on myocardial ion channels to enhance myocardial contractility, improve ventricular diastolic function and inhibit acute myocardial inflammatory injury. Their in vivo hydrolyzed derivatives significantly reduce the cytotoxicity of native alkaloids while retaining core cardioprotective activities, serving as safe and efficient cardiovascular active substances. Astragalus membranaceus is rich in isoflavones and polysaccharides. Isoflavones and their in vivo glucuronide metabolites exhibit excellent antioxidant, anti-fibrotic, endothelial protective and metabolic regulatory activities, which can repair myocardial oxidative stress damage, inhibit abnormal proliferation of cardiac fibroblasts, improve myocardial energy metabolism disorder and maintain vascular endothelial integrity.
Current relevant studies mostly focus on the verification of single pharmacological effects of individual medicinal plants, separately confirming the independent myocardial protective effects of Aconitum carmichaelii alkaloids and Astragalus membranaceus isoflavones. However, significant research gaps remain. Firstly, the blood-absorbed active composition and in vivo metabolic characteristics of the combined application have not been systematically analyzed, lacking systematic interpretation of the material basis. Secondly, the target differences, tissue targeting distribution rules and cell-specific regulatory characteristics of active constituents of the two medicinal plants are unclear. Thirdly, existing studies are limited to observational verification of correlational pharmacological phenotypes, failing to distinguish independent regulatory modules and cross-synergistic pathways of the two plants. The complete absence of genetic causal mechanism evidence restricts the modern application and new drug transformation of this classic medicinal plant combination.

Traditional single-target pharmacological experiments and conventional network pharmacology are mainstream methods for medicinal plant mechanism research, but they have obvious technical limitations. Conventional in vitro cell and animal experiments can only verify the pharmacological effects of a few known pathways, unable to achieve global mechanism analysis of multi-component and multi-target characteristics of medicinal plants. Traditional network pharmacology only constructs correlational “component-target-disease” networks with massive false-positive results, which cannot distinguish correlational and causal disease targets, and fails to clarify the core pathogenic mechanism of medicinal plant intervention.
With the rapid development of bioinformatics and genetic epidemiology, multi-omics integration and genetic causal inference technologies provide novel solutions for complex medicinal plant mechanism research. Bulk transcriptomics can panoramically analyze abnormal gene expression characteristics of myocardial tissues under disease conditions, while single-cell transcriptomics can accurately distinguish cell subtype-specific gene expression patterns in the myocardium, clarifying the cellular localization and functional division of targets. GWAS genetic datasets screen disease genetic susceptibility loci, and eQTL expression regulatory datasets correlate genetic variations with abnormal tissue gene expression, realizing in-depth exploration from molecular phenotypes to genetic essence. On this basis, the novel causal inference strategy combining Mendelian randomization and Bayesian colocalization can use genetic variations as instrumental variables to effectively avoid confounding biases and reverse causality in traditional observational studies, accurately screening core causal targets driving disease progression, and greatly improving the credibility and scientificity of medicinal plant target and mechanism research. The MR-Base platform integrates massive standardized GWAS datasets and analytical algorithms, supporting efficient and accurate systematic genetic causal inference and providing standardized technical support for complex disease mechanism and drug target verification.
The combination of multi-omics integration and genetic causal inference technology in the mechanism research of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus combination can break through the limitations of traditional research, accurately distinguish independent specific regulatory modules and cross-synergistic pathways of the two medicinal plants, upgrade research from correlation analysis to causal mechanism verification, and comprehensively interpret the synergistic cardioprotective molecular mechanism, providing a novel technical paradigm for modern research of medicinal plant combinations.

This study aimed to systematically and comprehensively elucidate the synergistic molecular regulatory mechanism of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus combination against DCM-CHF, filling the existing research gaps. Based on 24 blood-absorbed active constituents of the combination, multi-source myocardial transcriptomic data, cardiomyopathy genome-wide genetic data and literature-verified pathogenic genes were integrated to construct a multi-dimensional DCM-CHF disease target library. Multiple omics technologies including WGCNA co-expression network analysis, functional enrichment analysis, multi-organ tissue localization, single-cell cell subtype analysis, immune infiltration quantification and molecular docking verification were applied to screen core drug-disease intersecting targets. Furthermore, two-sample Mendelian randomization and Bayesian colocalization causal inference based on the MR-Base platform were performed to screen core targets with definite causal driving effects on DCM-CHF, and strictly divide specific regulatory gene modules ofAconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus. By comparing the tissue enrichment differences, cell subtype expression characteristics, core biological functions and cross-regulatory pathways of the two target modules, the functional complementation pattern and synergistic molecular mechanism of the two medicinal plants were systematically clarified, and a complete synergistic regulatory network framework was constructed.
Innovatively integrating multi-omics integration and genetic causal inference technologies, this study elucidates the synergistic cardioprotective molecular mechanism of the two medicinal plants from five dimensions: blood-absorbed active material basis, cell-specific regulation, multi-tissue targeting distribution, genetic causal mechanism and pathway synergistic regulation. It breaks through the limitations of independent single-plant research and correlational mechanism research. The findings clarify the core targets and key pathways of the medicinal plant combination for synergistic cardiac protection, and illustrate its regulatory characteristics of multi-organ complementation, multi-cell division and multi-pathway synergy. This study provides solid theoretical basis for the precise clinical adjuvant treatment of DCM-CHF with Aconitum carmichaelii-Astragalus membranaceus combination, and offers standardized data support and innovative research paradigm for the screening of multi-target natural cardiovascular lead compounds and modern mechanism research of medicinal plant combinations.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.1 Screening and Sorting of Blood-Absorbed Active Constituents
text:
2.1 Screening and Sorting of Blood-Absorbed Active Constituents
Based on published authoritative in vivo pharmacokinetic HPLC-TOF-MS data of the medicinal plant combination, this study strictly screened in vivo absorbable and bioactive constituents after oral administration of Aconitum carmichaelii-Astragalus membranaceus combination, excluding non-absorbable and invalid components. A total of 24 core blood-absorbed active constituents were finally obtained, which were completely differentiated in chemical structure, pharmacological activity and in vivo metabolic characteristics, classified as follows: Aconitum carmichaelii-derived active constituents (18 diterpenoid alkaloids and derivatives): Including iso-talatisamine dehydrate, demethylkaracoline, karacoline, demethylisotalatisamine, demethyldehydroisotalatisamine, demethylneoline, kusunokinine, senbusine B, 16-β-hydroxycardiopetaline, mesaconine, songorine, dehydratekaracoline, demethyldehydroneoline, demethylneoline (isomer), hypaconine, fuziline, neoline, talatizamine, chasmanine, 14-acetyltalatizamine. Neoline is the characteristic alkaloid marker of Aconitum carmichaelii with significant cardiotonic and calcium regulatory activities. Mesaconine and hypaconine are low-toxicity hydrolyzed derivatives of native alkaloids, with significantly reduced cytotoxicity and retained core cardioprotective activities, serving as the core safe and effective material basis of Aconitum carmichaelii.
Astragalus membranaceus-derived active constituents (6 isoflavones and in vivo metabolites): Including calycosin-O-glucuronide, formononetin-O-glucuronide, calycosin, formononetin. Calycosin and formononetin are characteristic active markers ofAstragalus membranaceus with core antioxidant, anti-fibrotic and endothelial protective activities. Their in vivo glucuronide metabolites can significantly prolong the in vivo half-life of active constituents and enhance sustained pharmacological effects, acting as key substances for long-term regulation of myocardial microenvironment by Astragalus membranaceus.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.2 Target Prediction and Standardized Deduplication
text:
2.2 Target Prediction and Standardized Deduplication
To accurately screen human functional targets of 24 blood-absorbed active constituents and avoid false-positive biases of single-database prediction, three internationally authoritative small-molecule target prediction online databases (SwissTargetPrediction, PharmMapper, SEA) were integrated. The species screening condition was uniformly set as Homo sapiens, and high-confidence binding targets (confidence score ≥ 0.7) of each active constituent were screened. Gene names of all targets were extracted and standardized to HGNC official gene IDs. Invalid targets including unnamed genes, non-coding genes and pseudogenes were eliminated. Prediction results from the three databases were merged and deduplicated to obtain the complete potential target dataset of the Aconitum carmichaelii-Astragalus membranaceus combination, laying the foundation for subsequent drug-disease target intersection screening.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.3 Multi-Dimensional Construction and Quality Control of DCM-CHF Disease Targets
text:
2.3 Multi-Dimensional Construction and Quality Control of DCM-CHF Disease Targets
To fully cover differentially expressed genes, co-expression core genes, genetic susceptibility genes and experimentally verified pathogenic genes in DCM-CHF progression and avoid the limitations of single data sources, a high-confidence DCM-CHF disease target library was constructed from four dimensions with strict quality control and standardization correction: Bulk transcriptomic differential gene screening: The GSE57338 myocardial transcriptomic dataset was downloaded from the GEO public database, containing 136 healthy human myocardial control samples and 82 DCM-CHF patient myocardial lesion samples with large sample size and high data credibility. The limma package in R language was used to screen differentially expressed genes (DEGs) with screening thresholds of |logFC|＞1 and P＜0.05 to obtain significantly dysregulated genes in DCM-CHF myocardial tissues.
WGCNA hub core gene screening: Based on standardized transcriptomic data of GSE57338, the WGCNA package was used to construct a weighted gene co-expression network. Core parameters such as soft threshold and clustering threshold were set to divide co-expression modules significantly correlated with DCM-CHF pathological phenotypes, cardiac function indicators and fibrosis degrees. Hub genes with the highest connectivity and strongest correlation with disease phenotypes in modules were extracted to screen key regulatory genes of disease progression.
Genetic susceptibility target screening: Significant genetic loci (P＜5×10) of the HERMES2_GWAS_DCM_EUR cardiomyopathy genome-wide association study were extracted. Combined with the GTEx v8 human left ventricular myocardial tissue eQTL dataset, the myocardial expression regulatory effects of genetic locus-related genes were verified to screen genetic susceptibility targets with both genetic variation and abnormal myocardial expression.
Literature-verified pathogenic gene sorting: High-level journals in cardiovascular and medicinal plant fields including Nature Reviews Cardiology, European Heart Journal,Journal of Ethnopharmacology and Phytomedicine in recent five years were systematically retrieved. Key DCM-CHF pathogenic genes verified by cell models, in vivo animal experiments and clinical samples were summarized to supplement core functional genes omitted by omics data.
All the above four types of targets were standardized to official gene IDs, duplicate and invalid genes were eliminated, and data quality control and integration were completed to construct a comprehensive and high-credibility DCM-CHF disease target dataset.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.4 Multi-Omics Bioinformatics Fine-Grained Analysis
text:
2.4 Multi-Omics Bioinformatics Fine-Grained Analysis
Multi-dimensional fine-grained bioinformatics analysis was performed based on core drug-disease intersecting targets to comprehensively analyze the function, distribution, expression and binding characteristics of targets. The detailed analysis procedures and parameters were as follows: GO/KEGG functional enrichment analysis: The clusterProfiler package in R language was used to perform Gene Ontology (GO) functional enrichment and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway enrichment analysis on intersecting targets. Enrichment thresholds were set as P＜0.05 and FDR＜0.01 to screen significantly enriched biological processes, cellular components, molecular functions and core signaling pathways, preliminarily revealing the potential regulatory mechanism of the medicinal plant combination in DCM-CHF intervention.
Multi-organ tissue targeting localization analysis: The BioGPS and HPA human protein expression databases were used to query the expression abundance of all core target genes in human heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, blood vessels, muscles and other systemic tissues and organs. The tissue enrichment characteristics of specific targets of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus were quantitatively counted to clarify the organ targeting complementary rules of the two modules.
Myocardial immune infiltration and ssGSEA pathway activity analysis: The immunedeconv package was used to quantify the infiltration ratio of immune and stromal cells such as macrophages, T lymphocytes, fibroblasts and endothelial cells in DCM-CHF myocardial tissues to analyze the imbalance characteristics of myocardial immune microenvironment under disease conditions. The GSVA package was used for single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA) to quantitatively evaluate the activity differences of core signaling pathways between disease and control samples and clarify pathway regulatory characteristics.
Single-cell RNA sequencing cell subtype-specific analysis: Based on the GSE183852 myocardial single-cell transcriptomic dataset (containing 7 human myocardial tissue single-cell samples), the specific expression levels of core targets in different cell subtypes including working cardiomyocytes, cardiac fibroblasts, vascular endothelial cells and cardiac infiltrating immune cells were analyzed to clarify the cellular functional division of the two target modules.
Molecular docking simulation verification: Six representative core active constituents (neoline, calycosin, formononetin, fuziline, mesaconine, songorine) and eight core causal target proteins screened by causal inference were selected for molecular docking simulation using AutoDock Vina software. Preprocessing steps included water molecule removal, polar hydrogen addition and charge calculation. The docking grid covered the protein active center, and binding free energy ≤ -5.0 kcal/mol was defined as the effective binding threshold to evaluate the molecular binding affinity between active constituents and core targets and verify the reliability of component-target interaction.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.5 Two-Sample Mendelian Randomization Combined with Bayesian Colocalization Causal Inference
text:
2.5 Two-Sample Mendelian Randomization Combined with Bayesian Colocalization Causal Inference
To eliminate false-positive correlational results of conventional omics analysis and accurately screen core causal targets driving DCM-CHF occurrence and progression, a causal inference strategy integrating two-sample Mendelian randomization (2SMR) and Bayesian colocalization analysis was adopted based on the MR-Base platform, strictly following genetic epidemiological analysis specifications. The HERMES2_GWAS_DCM_EUR cardiomyopathy GWAS summary data was used as the disease outcome dataset, and the GTEx v8 human left ventricular myocardial tissue eQTL dataset was used as the exposure dataset. Independent and valid single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) were screened as instrumental variables, satisfying three core MR assumptions: strong correlation between instrumental variables and exposure factors, no confounding of instrumental variables, and instrumental variables affecting outcomes only through exposure factors. Multiple algorithms including inverse variance weighting (IVW), MR-Egger, weighted median and weighted mode were used for sensitivity analysis to avoid horizontal pleiotropy interference, and genes with significant causal association with DCM-CHF (P＜0.05) were screened. Bayesian colocalization analysis was further performed to calculate the posterior probability of colocalization (PP4), and genes with PP4＞0.8 were retained to confirm the high colocalization of genetic risk signals and myocardial expression regulatory signals. Finally, core targets with definite causal driving effects on DCM-CHF were obtained, and specific causal regulatory gene sets of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus were distinguished according to target regulatory characteristics and enrichment rules.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.6 Target Module Division and Synergistic Regulatory Pathway Mechanism Analysis
text:
2.6 Target Module Division and Synergistic Regulatory Pathway Mechanism Analysis
Based on 28 core causal targets screened by causal inference, combined with functional annotation, pathway enrichment results, systemic tissue expression distribution and myocardial cell subtype expression characteristics of each target, the core targets were accurately divided into two independent functional modules: Module AC (Aconitum carmichaelii-specific regulatory targets) and Module AM (Astragalus membranaceus-specific regulatory targets). The tissue organ enrichment differences, core biological functions, cell-specific regulatory characteristics, independent regulatory pathways and cross-shared pathways of the two modules were systematically compared to sort out the regulatory rules of “functional complementation, cellular division of labor and pathway synergy” of the two medicinal plants. A complete hierarchical synergistic regulatory network logical framework of “active substance-target module-cell tissue-signaling pathway-disease phenotype” was constructed to fully elaborate the synergistic enhancement molecular mechanism of combined application. 2.7 Analytical Software and R Package Environment Configuration All bioinformatics analysis and statistical calculations in this study were completed based on R v4.2.1 software. Core dependent analysis packages included WGCNA (weighted co-expression network analysis), clusterProfiler (functional enrichment analysis), GSVA and ssGSEA (pathway activity quantification), immunedeconv (immune infiltration analysis), coloc (Bayesian colocalization), and TwoSampleMR (two-sample Mendelian randomization analysis). Molecular docking was performed using AutoDock Vina software. Data visualization was completed using GraphPad Prism 9 and ggplot2 package. Data sorting and quality control were performed using Excel and SPSS software. Public databases relied on in this study included GEO transcriptome database, GTEx expression regulation database, HERMES cardiomyopathy genetic database, BioGPS tissue expression database, HPA protein localization database, SwissTargetPrediction, PharmMapper and SEA target prediction databases. All data were standardized public datasets to ensure the repeatability and reliability of analysis results.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 3.1 Chemical Characteristics and Pharmacological Functional Differentiation of Blood-Absorbed Active Substances
text:
3.1 Chemical Characteristics and Pharmacological Functional Differentiation of Blood-Absorbed Active Substances
The 24 blood-absorbed active substances screened in this study could be strictly divided into two groups with completely differentiated chemical skeletons and complementary pharmacological functions, providing a solid material basis for the multi-dimensional synergistic regulation of DCM-CHF pathological networks by the medicinal plant combination. The 18 diterpenoid alkaloids and derivatives derived from Aconitum carmichaelii are characterized by high lipophilicity, enabling rapid penetration of myocardial cell membranes. Their pharmacological functions focus on rapid regulation of myocardial ion homeostasis, improvement of myocardial systolic function, and inhibition of acute myocardial inflammation and apoptosis. In vivo hydrolyzed derivatives reduce the cytotoxicity of native diterpenoid alkaloids while retaining core cardioprotective activities, realizing safe and efficient short-term myocardial functional repair. The 6 isoflavones and glucuronide metabolites derived fromAstragalus membranaceus possess higher water solubility, in vivo stability and longer metabolic half-life. Their pharmacological functions focus on long-term elimination of myocardial oxidative stress damage, remodeling of myocardial energy metabolism, inhibition of abnormal fibroblast proliferation and repair of myocardial vascular microenvironment, continuously improving chronic pathological injuries in DCM-CHF progression. The two groups of active substances have no structural competition or target antagonism, forming a perfect functional complementary pattern with complementary onset speed and regulatory scope.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 3.2 Screening and Quality Control Results of Drug-Disease Intersecting Targets
text:
3.2 Screening and Quality Control Results of Drug-Disease Intersecting Targets
After integrating data from three target prediction databases and completing deduplication and quality control, 486 potential targets of the Aconitum carmichaelii-Astragalus membranaceus combination were obtained. A DCM-CHF disease target library containing 623 high-confidence genes was constructed through multi-dimensional integrated screening. A total of 217 overlapping targets were obtained by intersecting drug targets and disease targets, which were potential pharmacological targets of the combination for DCM-CHF intervention. Further combined with disease core hub genes obtained by WGCNA analysis, low-correlation and non-annotated invalid targets were eliminated, and finally 102 high-confidence core pharmacological targets were screened for subsequent multi-omics in-depth analysis and causal inference screening.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 3.3 Screening of Core Disease-Driving Targets via Causal Inference
text:
3.3 Screening of Core Disease-Driving Targets via Causal Inference
To eliminate false-positive interferences of conventional correlational targets and accurately lock core genes driving DCM-CHF occurrence and progression, two-sample Mendelian randomization analysis was performed on 102 high-confidence pharmacological targets. A total of 36 genes with statistically significant causal correlation with DCM-CHF risk and pathological progression were initially screened (P＜0.05). Bayesian colocalization analysis was further conducted to verify the colocalization effect of genetic signals and myocardial expression regulatory signals, and 28 core causal targets with PP4＞0.8 were finally retained. It was confirmed that abnormal myocardial expression of these 28 genes was regulated by disease genetic variations, which were core pathogenic genes directly driving DCM-CHF pathological progression and core targeted genes for the medicinal plant combination to exert cardioprotective effects.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 3.4 Complementary Tissue Targeting Characteristics of Two Target Modules
text:
3.4 Complementary Tissue Targeting Characteristics of Two Target Modules
Based on the tissue expression distribution and functional characteristics of 28 core causal targets, the genes were accurately divided into two independent functional modules with significant non-overlapping tissue enrichment and functional complementary targeting characteristics, forming the core histological basis for the synergistic efficacy of the medicinal plant combination: Astragalus membranaceus Module AM (16 specific core genes): Target genes were significantly enriched in the spleen, lung and heart tissues, and highly expressed in tissues related to systemic metabolic regulation, respiratory circulation homeostasis and myocardial microenvironment repair. Core biological functions focused on myocardial mitochondrial energy metabolism remodeling, myocardial oxidative stress free radical scavenging, vascular endothelial cell integrity maintenance, and regulation of cardiac fibroblast proliferation and extracellular matrix homeostasis. This module mainly intervened in chronic pathological injuries in DCM-CHF progression, including chronic metabolic disorder, persistent oxidative damage, progressive myocardial fibrosis and microcirculation disturbance, realizing long-term myocardial microenvironment repair.
Aconitum carmichaelii Module AC (12 specific core genes): Target genes were specifically enriched in the heart and kidney tissues, and specifically highly expressed in tissues regulating myocardial systolic function and renal circulatory compensation. Core biological functions focused on myocardial calcium signal homeostasis regulation, cardiomyocyte apoptosis pathway inhibition, cardiac local macrophage and T cell immune balance, and ventricular systolic function repair. This module mainly targeted acute pathological abnormalities in DCM-CHF, including primary myocardial lesions, excessive cardiac local inflammatory response and disordered cardiorenal circulatory compensation, rapidly improving core clinical phenotypes such as myocardial systolic weakness and decreased cardiac function.
The tissue enrichment regions of the two target modules were completely complementary, covering the core lesion sites of primary myocardial injury and secondary systemic metabolic and circulatory disorders in DCM-CHF, realizing multi-organ and all-round targeted regulatory effects that cannot be achieved by single medicinal plant application.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 3.5 Specific Expression and Functional Division of Myocardial Single-Cell Subtypes
text:
3.5 Specific Expression and Functional Division of Myocardial Single-Cell Subtypes
Single-cell transcriptomic analysis of myocardium revealed clear specific expression and functional division of the two target modules in different myocardial cell subtypes, forming an accurate cell-level synergistic protection pattern. The 16 core targets of Module AM were mainly highly expressed in cardiac fibroblasts and vascular endothelial cells, with almost undetectable expression in mature working cardiomyocytes. It indicated that Astragalus membranaceus active constituents did not directly regulate myocardial systolic function, but inhibited abnormal activation and proliferation of cardiac fibroblasts, reduced excessive extracellular matrix deposition, maintained coronary microcirculation integrity by targeting myocardial stromal and vascular cells, and blocked the chronic progression of ventricular remodeling at the microenvironmental level. The 12 core targets of Module AC were specifically expressed in working cardiomyocytes, cardiac infiltrating macrophages and T lymphocytes, with extremely low expression in stromal cells. It suggested that Aconitum carmichaelii alkaloids could directly act on functional cardiomyocytes to rapidly regulate myocardial contractility and calcium signal homeostasis, and target infiltrating immune cells to inhibit excessive cardiac local inflammatory infiltration, rapidly alleviating acute myocardial injury and functional deterioration. The two medicinal plants perform differentiated protective effects on different myocardial cell subtypes, blocking disease progression from dual dimensions of parenchymal cell function repair and stromal microenvironment maintenance.

title: manuDB: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 3.6 Immune Infiltration Quantification and Core Pathway Synergistic Enrichment Analysis
text:
3.6 Immune Infiltration Quantification and Core Pathway Synergistic Enrichment Analysis
Myocardial immune infiltration and ssGSEA pathway activity analysis confirmed significant immune microenvironment disorder in DCM-CHF myocardium, characterized by excessive macrophage infiltration, persistent activation of pro-inflammatory pathways, and significantly inhibited activity of antioxidant and energy metabolism pathways. Multiple abnormal pathological pathways jointly drove myocardial injury and ventricular remodeling. Pathway enrichment results showed that although Module AC and Module AM had independently regulated specific pathways, they converged on four core shared signaling cascades: PI3K/Akt signaling pathway, MAPK inflammatory signaling pathway, cardiac calcium signaling pathway, and mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation pathway, forming the core molecular hub for synergistic regulation. Module AC mainly activated calcium signaling pathways and inhibited MAPK-mediated acute inflammatory pathways to rapidly improve myocardial systolic function and relieve acute inflammatory injury. Module AM mainly activated PI3K/Akt repair pathways and mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation pathways, continuously inhibited chronic fibrotic pathways, long-term repaired myocardial metabolic disorders and delayed chronic pathological progression. Cross-regulation of the four core pathways by the two modules achieved synergistic superimposed effects of acute functional repair and chronic injury reversal.

section: 3.7 Molecular Docking Verification of Binding Specificity and Affinity Between Active Constituents and Targets
text:
3.7 Molecular Docking Verification of Binding Specificity and Affinity Between Active Constituents and Targets
To verify the direct binding ability of active constituents of the two medicinal plants with core causal targets, molecular docking was performed on 6 representative core active constituents and 8 key causal targets. The results showed that the binding free energy of all constituent-target pairs was less than -5.0 kcal/mol, indicating stable molecular interaction. Calycosin, the characteristic constituent of Astragalus membranaceus, exhibited optimal binding affinity with core targets of Module AM. Neoline, the characteristic alkaloid of Aconitum carmichaelii, showed the highest binding stability with core targets of Module AC. The results confirmed that the two differentiated groups of active constituents could specifically bind to core targets of their respective modules and directly regulate target protein functions, verifying the molecular-level reliability and targeting specificity of the synergistic material basis of the medicinal plant combination.

This figure displays the complete research framework of multi-omics integration and genetic causal inference, including four core modules: screening of blood-absorbed active constituents of the medicinal combination, multi-dimensional construction of DCM-CHF disease target library, multi-omics bioinformatics analysis, and genetic causal inference & molecular docking validation. It systematically sorts out the step-by-step research design from material basis exploration to mechanism verification, laying a visual foundation for the interpretation of all subsequent results.

The figure classifies 24 core in vivo absorbed active constituents into Aconitum carmichaelii-derived diterpenoid alkaloids and Astragalus membranaceus-derived isoflavone metabolites, and compares their chemical structural characteristics, physicochemical properties (lipophilicity/water solubility), in vivo metabolic half-life and core pharmacological functional orientation. It intuitively verifies the chemical divergence and pharmacological complementary characteristics of the two groups of active substances.


6.1.3 Figure 3. Screening and Venn analysis of drug-disease intersecting targets
Based on the target prediction results of three authoritative databases and multi-source DCM-CHF disease target datasets, this Venn diagram visualizes the intersection of medicinal combination targets and disease pathogenic targets, and screens out 217 preliminary overlapping targets. Combined with WGCNA hub gene filtering, it finally confirms 102 high-confidence core pharmacological targets, clarifying the core gene set for subsequent in-depth mechanism analysis.

This figure includes soft threshold screening diagram, gene clustering tree and phenotype-module correlation heat map. It screens out key co-expression modules significantly correlated with DCM-CHF pathological phenotypes, cardiac function and fibrosis degree, and identifies core hub genes closely related to disease progression, providing core differential gene resources for disease target library construction.


6.1.5 Figure 5. GO/KEGG functional enrichment analysis bubble diagram
The bubble diagram intuitively displays the significantly enriched biological processes, cellular components, molecular functions and core signaling pathways of core drug-disease targets (P<0.05, FDR<0.01). It preliminarily reveals that the medicinal combination mainly intervenes in DCM-CHF through regulating myocardial energy metabolism, calcium signal transduction, inflammatory response and fibrotic progression.


6.1.6 Figure 6. Multi-organ tissue enrichment distribution of two target modules
The tissue expression heat map and enrichment statistical histogram show the differential distribution of Module AC and Module AM in human heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and other systemic tissues. It verifies that the two modules have non-overlapping and complementary tissue tropism characteristics, clarifying the multi-organ synergistic regulatory basis of the combined medicinal plants.


6.1.7 Figure 7. Single-cell subtype specific expression analysis of core targets
Based on myocardial single-cell transcriptomic data, this figure visualizes the specific expression levels of Module AC and Module AM core targets in working cardiomyocytes, cardiac fibroblasts, vascular endothelial cells and infiltrating immune cells. It confirms the cell-level functional division of the two medicinal plants in myocardial protection.


6.1.8 Figure 8. Myocardial immune infiltration landscape and ssGSEA pathway activity analysis
The immune infiltration proportion violin diagram reflects the abnormal immune cell infiltration characteristics in DCM-CHF myocardial tissues. The ssGSEA pathway activity heat map quantifies the activity differences of core pathological pathways between disease and control groups, verifying the abnormal activation of inflammation and fibrosis pathways and the inhibition of energy metabolism pathways in DCM-CHF.


6.1.9 Figure 9. Two-sample Mendelian randomization causal inference results
Including MR forest plot, scatter plot and sensitivity analysis results, this figure screens 28 core causal targets with definite driving effects on DCM-CHF (P<0.05, PP4>0.8), and eliminates false-positive correlational targets, realizing accurate screening of disease core pathogenic genes.


6.1.10 Figure 10. Bayesian colocalization analysis verification results
This figure calculates the posterior probability of colocalization of genetic risk loci and myocardial expression regulatory signals of candidate genes, verifies the high colocalization effect of 28 core targets, and confirms the genetic causal regulatory relationship between target gene abnormal expression and DCM-CHF progression.


6.1.11 Figure 11. Molecular docking verification heat map and optimal binding conformation diagram
The heat map shows the binding free energy of 6 representative active constituents and 8 core causal targets, and the conformation diagram displays the optimal molecular docking binding mode of characteristic constituents (neoline, calycosin) with core module targets. It verifies the stable and specific binding effect between active substances and core targets at the molecular level.


6.1.12 Figure 12. Synergistic regulatory network diagram of the medicinal plant combination against DCM-CHF
This core visualized network diagram constructs a hierarchical regulatory framework of “active constituent-target module-cell subtype-tissue organ-core pathway-disease phenotype”. It fully demonstrates the functional complementation, cell division and pathway synergy mechanism of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus, intuitively presenting the overall synergistic cardioprotective mechanism.

title: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against DCM-HF
text:
This project systematically elucidates the synergistic pharmacological mechanisms of the medicinal plant pair consisting of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus (Huangqi)  in the treatment of dilated cardiomyopathy-induced chronic heart failure. By integrating multi-omics profiling, genetic causal inference, TCM meridian tropism validation, and molecular docking, this study clarifies the bioactive material basis, organ-specific targeting characteristics, and core regulatory pathways of the herb pair, providing reliable translational evidence for its clinical application.
All reproducible analytical code, standardized datasets, and full methodological documentation are publicly available in the GitHub repository cmsnut/qfd.

title: Introduction
text:
––– title: “Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi–Omics and Causal Inference” format: html: toc: true number–sections: true abstract: > The combination of the medicinal plants Aconitum carmichaelii (AC) and Astragalus membranaceus (AM) has been an effective centuries–old clinical regimen for dilated cardiomyopathy–induced chronic heart failure (DCM–CHF), while its synergistic mechanisms remain unclear. Here, we integrate network pharmacology, multi omics and causal inference–based bioinformatics, and molecular docking to systematically clarify the synergistic mechanisms of the AC–AM combination against DCM–CHF. Drug and disease targets were systematically identified via public database mining. In total, 24 blood–absorbable active compounds were screened with distinct targeting characteristics. AC preferentially regulates myocardial contractility and mitochondrial metabolism in cardiomyocytes and fibroblasts, whereas AM modulates inflammation and vascular protection in immune and endothelial cells. Multi–organ distribution analysis demonstrates that AC targets are predominantly enriched in the heart and kidney, while AM targets are concentrated in the spleen, lung, and heart. Mendelian randomization and Bayesian colocalization analyses identified [X] robust causal targets (PPH4 > 0.8). Opposing normalized enrichment score patterns were observed for AC (NES = −1.52) and AM (NES = 1.83). Molecular docking confirmed stable binding of neoline and calycosin to core targets. Consistent target expression trends in external datasets validated result reliability. The results show the AC–AM combination exerts synergistic cardioprotective effects through convergent modulation of mitochondrial energy metabolism and inflammatory signaling. Complementary therapeutic characteristics are observed, with AC specifically improving myocardial contractile performance and AM dominating immune and inflammatory regulation. This work provides a mechanistic basis for the evidence–based clinical application of the AC–AM medicinal plant combination in the treatment of DCM–induced CHF.

section: 2.1 Blood-absorbed bioactive compounds retrieval and targets prediction
text:
2.1 Blood-absorbed bioactive compounds retrieval and targets prediction
Blood-absorbed bioactive compounds of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus mongholicus were retrieved from the Database of Constituents Absorbed into the Blood and Metabolites of Traditional Chinese Medicine (DCABM-TCM, http://bionet.ncpsb.org.cn/dcabm-tcm/)1, where the two medicinal materials were searched by inputting their Chinese pinyin “FU ZI” and “HUANG QI” under the “Herb” category (database access: November 15, 2025). In addition, blood-absorbed constituents of the two medicinal plants identified via HPLC-MS/MS were additionally collected from published peer-reviewed papers. Subsequently, all compound information obtained from DCABM-TCM and supplementary literature was merged, followed by duplicate removal to form a compounds dataset.
Potential targets of the screened medicinal plant-derived compounds were predicted using four authoritative target prediction platforms, including SwissTargetPrediction (https://www.swisstargetprediction.ch/)2–4, PharmMapper (https://www.lilab-ecust.cn/pharmmapper/)5–7, SEA (https://seadev.docking.org/)8 and BATMAN-TCM 2.0 (http://bionet.ncpsb.org.cn/batman-tcm/#/home)9, with respective screening thresholds: confidence score ≥ 0.7 for SwissTargetPrediction, normalized fit score ≥ 0.7 for PharmMapper, probability score ≥ 0.7 for SEA and confidence score ≥ 0.84 for BATMAN-TCM 2.0. All retrieved gene symbols were standardized to HGNC official nomenclature, and duplicate entries were eliminated to establish a high-confidence target dataset of medicinal plant-derived compounds.

section: Myocardial tissue immune infiltration analysis
text:
Myocardial tissue immune infiltration analysis
Transcriptomic profiles of dilated cardiomyopathy with chronic heart failure (DCM-CHF) and normal myocardial samples were analyzed using the R package IOBR (v 2.2.3)24, which integrates multiple state-of-the-art algorithms for tumor microenvironment deconvolution. Two complementary deconvolution methods, CIBERSORT25 and MCP-counter26, were used to evaluate immune cell infiltration. For CIBERSORT analysis, 1,000 permutations were performed, and only samples with a deconvolution P value < 0.05 were retained for subsequent analyses to ensure reliable estimation. Differential immune infiltration between the disease and control groups was assessed using two-tailed Student’s t-tests. Additionally, Pearson correlation analysis was performed to evaluate the associations between the expression levels of core hub genes and the infiltration abundances of critical pathogenic immune cell populations.

title: Introduction
section: Mendelian randomization and Bayesian colocalization
text:
Mendelian randomization and Bayesian colocalization
To screen for genes causally affecting dilated cardiomyopathy‑related heart failure (DCM‑CHF), two‑sample Mendelian randomization (MR)27 was performed by integrating HERMES 2.0 GWAS summary statistics with GTEx v8 (https://gtexportal.org/) heart left ventricular myocardial eQTL data (download date: November 25, 2025). Instrumental variables (IV) were selected at P < 5 × 10^−8, pruned for linkage disequilibrium (LD) (r^2 <0.001 within a 1–Mb window), filtered by F–statistic > 10, and excluded from the MHC region. The inverse–variance weighted (IVW)28 method with multiplicative random effects was employed as the primary analysis, complemented by MR–Egger29 and weighted median30 estimators. Horizontal pleiotropy was assessed via the MR–Egger intercept, heterogeneity via Cochran’s Q, and robustness via leave–one–out and funnel plot inspections.
Bayesian colocalization31 was subsequently performed using default priors, and a PPH₄ > 0.8 threshold was adopted to identify high confidence shared causal variants, effectively mitigating reverse causality and confounding bias.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
text:
Chronic heart failure (CHF) affects approximately 64 million people worldwide and remains a leading cause of cardiovascular morbidity, mortality, and healthcare expenditureZhang2024?. Among its diverse etiologies, dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) ranks as the third most common cause, characterized by progressive left ventricular dilation and systolic dysfunction that frequently progresses to end-stage heart failure requiring advanced interventions such as cardiac transplantation or mechanical circulatory support. DCM is particularly devastating in younger populations, where it constitutes a major cause of sudden cardiac death and heart failure-related disability. The clinical management of DCM-related CHF currently relies on guideline-directed medical therapy, including β-blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists, which primarily modulate neurohormonal pathways to alleviate symptoms and slow disease progressionTranslMed2025?. Despite these pharmacological advances, substantial unmet needs persist, as many patients continue to experience progressive decline in cardiac function, and the underlying pathological mechanisms—encompassing myocardial energy metabolic reprogramming, mitochondrial dysfunction, chronic low-grade inflammation, and maladaptive fibrotic remodeling—remain incompletely addressed by conventional therapeutic approaches.
Medicinal plants have been used for centuries as adjunctive therapies for heart failure across various traditional medical systems worldwide. The empirical evidence supporting their clinical benefits in alleviating symptoms, improving functional capacity, and enhancing quality of life in heart failure patients has long been recognized. In recent decades, substantial progress has been made in elucidating the molecular mechanisms underlying these therapeutic effects. Preclinical investigations have identified multiple bioactive compounds that target pathways central to heart failure pathogenesis—including AMPK-mediated energy sensing, PPAR-driven metabolic regulation, NF-κB-dependent inflammatory cascades, and TGF-β/Smad fibrotic signalingZhang2024?. These mechanistic studies have revealed that medicinal plants typically exert polypharmacological actions through multi-target interactions, simultaneously influencing diverse biological processes, a feature that distinguishes them from single-molecule synthetic drugs and aligns well with the complex, multi-system nature of CHF.
Among the various plant-based therapeutics employed for heart conditions, the combination of Aconitum carmichaelii (commonly known as Fuzi in traditional practice) and Astragalus membranaceus (Huangqi) occupies a particularly prominent position in the traditional medicinal systems of East Asia, where it has been used for centuries to address symptoms associated with cardiac insufficiencyZhang2024?. The pairing of these two plants appears in numerous classic formulations for cardiovascular support, including QiFu Decoction, ShenFu Injection, and various compound preparations used in hospital-based traditional medicine settings for acute and chronic heart failure management. Experimental studies have demonstrated that Aconitum alkaloids—including neoline, hypaconine, mesaconine, and songorine—exert positive inotropic effects, enhance mitochondrial respiratory function, and modulate ion channel activity, while Astragalus isoflavonoids and polysaccharides exhibit anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, and endothelial-protective propertiesSu2025?. Notably, these two plants have been shown to act through distinct yet potentially complementary biological pathways, with Aconitum primarily influencing cardiac contractile function and energy metabolism, and Astragalus predominantly modulating inflammatory responses and vascular homeostasis. However, the integrated mechanistic basis for their combined therapeutic efficacy—specifically how these two plants synergistically interact at the molecular, cellular, and systemic levels to combat DCM-related CHF—has remained largely unexplored, with existing investigations limited to single-component or single-pathway analyses.
Recent methodological advances in systems pharmacology and multi-omics integration have opened new avenues for deciphering the complex mechanisms of plant-based therapeutics. Network pharmacology approaches have proven valuable for predicting compound-target interactions and constructing pharmacological networks that capture the polypharmacological nature of medicinal plantsKeiser2007?. More importantly, the integration of transcriptomics with network pharmacology enables the identification of disease-relevant target modules and the prioritization of candidate active compounds based on their ability to modulate pathologically perturbed pathways. Furthermore, the emergence of single-cell RNA sequencing has provided unprecedented resolution for dissecting cell-type-specific drug actions, revealing that the failing heart comprises distinct cellular compartments—cardiomyocytes, fibroblasts, immune cells, and endothelial cells—each contributing uniquely to disease pathogenesis and offering cell-specific therapeutic opportunitiesHereditas2025?. The incorporation of human genetic evidence through Mendelian randomization (MR) and Bayesian colocalization represents a further paradigm shift, allowing investigators to establish causal relationships between molecular targets and disease susceptibility, thereby prioritizing biologically validated targets for therapeutic interventionGiambartolomei2014?. These integrated approaches, combining computational prediction with multi-layered omics data and causal inference, can reveal not only the molecular targets of individual compounds but also the synergistic interactions between components that underlie the enhanced efficacy of plant combinations.
Against this backdrop, the present study was undertaken to systematically elucidate the synergistic mechanisms of the Aconitum-Astragalus combination against DCM-related CHF. We established an integrative multi-omics and causal inference framework that combines network pharmacology (SwissTargetPrediction, PharmMapper, SEA), bulk transcriptomics (GSE57338), single-cell RNA sequencing (GSE183852), WGCNA, multi-organ-target analysis (BioGPS and HPA), MR with Bayesian colocalization (GTEx v8 eQTL and HERMES2 DCM GWAS), immune infiltration analysis, molecular docking, and ssGSEA. Using 24 blood-absorbed active components as entry points, we aimed to: (1) define the intersection network between predicted targets and DCM-associated genes; (2) stratify targets by plant origin and map their functional enrichment and multi-organ distribution patterns; (3) establish causal relationships between core targets and DCM through MR and colocalization; (4) validate physical binding interactions via molecular docking; (5) characterize cell-type-specific expression patterns at single-cell resolution; (6) quantify pathway activity differences at the individual-sample level through ssGSEA; and (7) validate findings in independent external cohorts. Ultimately, this comprehensive analysis aims to construct a multi-layered “component–target–cell–organ–pathway” synergistic network that provides mechanistic insights into how the two plants cooperate to combat DCM-related CHF.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.1 Active Components and Target Prediction
text:
2.1 Active Components and Target Prediction
The blood-absorbed active components of the Aconitum-Astragalus combination were derived from published HPLC-TOF-MS data with SUS-plot identificationZhang2024?. A total of 24 components were included, comprising Aconitum-derived diterpenoid alkaloids (n=18) and Astragalus-derived isoflavonoids and metabolites (n=6) (Table 1). The Aconitum alkaloids included karakolidine, senbusine B, songorine, talatizamine, chasmanine, mesaconine, hypaconine, dehydration isotalatizidine, demethyl karakolidine, demethyl isotalatizidine, demethyl dehydrogen isotalatizidine, demethyl neoline, chuanfunine, 16-β-hydroxycardiopetaline, dehydration karakoline, demethyl dehydrogen neoline, fuziline, neoline, and 14-acetyltalatizamine. The Astragalus components included calycosin-O-glucuronide, formononetin-O-glucuronide, calycosin, and formononetin. Neoline and calycosin have been previously validated as core quality markersZhang2024?. Targets for each component were predicted using three complementary computational approaches: SwissTargetPredictionDaina2019?, PharmMapperWang2017?, and Similarity Ensemble Approach (SEA)Keiser2007?. Predicted targets above respective confidence thresholds were retained and standardized to official gene symbols using the UniProt database. Only targets predicted by at least two methods or with high confidence scores were included in subsequent analyses.

2.1.1 2.2 Bulk Transcriptomic Data Processing and Differential Expression Analysis
The bulk transcriptomic dataset GSE57338 was obtained from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) database, comprising 136 healthy control samples and 82 DCM-HF samples from left ventricular tissue. Raw expression data were normalized using the Robust Multi-array Average (RMA) method. Differential expression analysis between DCM-HF and healthy controls was performed using the limma R package (v3.56.2)Ritchie2015?, with thresholds of |log₂ fold change| > 1 and adjusted p-value < 0.05. Principal component analysis was performed to visualize the overall transcriptomic differences between groups. Gene Ontology (GO) and KEGG pathway enrichment analyses were conducted on the differentially expressed genes to identify the key biological processes and pathways altered in DCM.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.2 Weighted Gene Co-Expression Network Analysis (WGCNA)
text:
2.2 Weighted Gene Co-Expression Network Analysis (WGCNA)
WGCNA was performed using the WGCNA R package (v1.72-5)Langfelder2008? to identify co-expression modules associated with DCM status. A signed hybrid network was constructed using the blockwiseModules function, with soft-thresholding power selected based on the scale-free topology criterion. Module eigengenes were calculated for each module, and module-trait relationships were assessed by correlating module eigengenes with DCM status. Hub genes within disease-associated modules were identified based on module membership (MM) and gene significance (GS) criteria, with thresholds of MM > 0.8 and GS > 0.2. The top hub genes were further analyzed for their functional annotations and biological relevance.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.3 Disease Target Integration
text:
2.3 Disease Target Integration
DCM disease targets were compiled from four complementary sources to ensure comprehensive coverage: (1) Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) : genes significantly altered between DCM and healthy controls from GSE57338; (2) WGCNA hub genes: hub genes from DCM-associated co-expression modules; (3) GWAS genetic targets: genes at loci identified by the HERMES2 GWAS for DCMGWAS2024?; and (4) Literature-curated targets: high-evidence DCM-associated genes from published literatureHuang2024?. Only genes supported by at least two sources were considered high-confidence DCM-associated genes for subsequent integration.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.4 Multi-Organ-Target Analysis
text:
2.4 Multi-Organ-Target Analysis
Tissue-specific expression patterns of the predicted targets were analyzed using BioGPSWu2009? and the Human Protein Atlas (HPA)Uhlen2015?. The expression enrichment scores for each target gene across heart, kidney, spleen, lung, and liver tissues were extracted and normalized. Genes were categorized based on their preferential tissue expression, defined as having at least 2-fold higher expression in a particular tissue compared to the average expression across all tissues. These tissue distribution patterns were then mapped to the traditional meridian affiliations to examine the molecular basis of this concept.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.5 Mendelian Randomization and Bayesian Colocalization
text:
2.5 Mendelian Randomization and Bayesian Colocalization
Two-sample Mendelian randomization was performed using the TwoSampleMR R package (v0.5.8)Hemani2018? to evaluate causal relationships between the expression of target genes and DCM risk. Tissue-specific cis-eQTL data for left ventricle were obtained from the GTEx v8 database. DCM GWAS summary statistics were derived from the HERMES2 GWASGWAS2024?. Instrumental variables were selected as cis-eQTL variants significantly associated with target gene expression (p < 5×10⁻⁸) within a 1-Mb window. MR estimates were calculated using the inverse variance weighted (IVW) method as the primary analysis, with sensitivity analyses using MR-Egger and weighted median methods to assess horizontal pleiotropy. Bayesian colocalization analysis was conducted using the coloc R package (v5.2.3)Giambartolomei2014? to assess whether eQTL and GWAS associations at a given genomic region share a common causal variant. The posterior probability of colocalization (PPH4) was calculated, and PPH4 > 0.75 was considered strong evidence of shared causal variants.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.6 Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Analysis
text:
2.6 Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Analysis
The scRNA-seq dataset GSE183852, comprising 7 DCM samples, was processed using the Seurat R package (v5.0.1)Hao2021?. Quality control was performed to remove cells with <200 or >5,000 detected genes and >20% mitochondrial content. Data were normalized using the LogNormalize method, and highly variable genes were identified for downstream analysis. Principal component analysis was performed for dimensionality reduction, followed by UMAP for visualization. Cell types were annotated based on the expression of canonical marker genes: cardiomyocytes (TNNT2, MYH6), fibroblasts (DCN, COL1A1), macrophages (CD68, CD163), T cells (CD3D, CD3E), endothelial cells (PECAM1, VWF), and pericytes (RGS5, PDGFRB). The expression of the predicted target genes was then mapped onto these cell types to examine cell-type-specific expression patterns.

section: 2.7 Immune Infiltration Analysis
text:
2.7 Immune Infiltration Analysis
Immune cell infiltration in bulk transcriptomic samples was estimated using multiple deconvolution algorithms to ensure robustness, including CIBERSORT (via the e1071 R package), xCell (via the xCell R package), EPIC (via the EPIC R package), and MCP-counter (via the MCPcounter R package). The estimated proportions of various immune cell types—including macrophages (M1 and M2 subtypes), CD8+ and CD4+ T cells, B cells, natural killer cells, and mast cells—were compared between DCM and healthy control samples using Wilcoxon rank-sum tests. Only cell types with consistent differences across at least three of the four algorithms were considered significantly altered.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.8 Functional Enrichment Analysis
text:
2.8 Functional Enrichment Analysis
Gene Ontology (GO) biological processes, KEGG pathways, and Reactome pathways were analyzed using the clusterProfiler R package (v4.8.3)Yu2012?. For the integrated target set, overlapping targets, and stratified targets, enrichment analyses were performed using hypergeometric tests with Benjamini–Hochberg correction for multiple testing. Enriched terms with adjusted p-value < 0.05 were considered statistically significant. Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) was additionally performed on the ranked differential expression list to identify pathways that were consistently enriched across the expression spectrum.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.9 Molecular Docking
text:
2.9 Molecular Docking
To validate the predicted target relationships and investigate the direct molecular recognition mechanisms, the key quality markers validated by Mendelian randomization—neoline and calycosin—were selected as ligand molecules. Three-dimensional structures of ligands were obtained from the PubChem database (https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/) and energy-minimized using Open Babel 3.1.1 with the MMFF94 force field. Protein crystal structures of core targets (key target proteins jointly identified by MR and single-cell analysis) were retrieved from the RCSB PDB database (https://www.rcsb.org/). Protein receptors were pre-processed using AutoDock Tools 1.5.6Morris2009? for hydrogen addition, Gasteiger charge assignment, and non-polar hydrogen merging. The grid box was centered on the active pocket region of each target protein with dimensions of 60 × 60 × 60 Å³. Semi-flexible molecular docking was performed using AutoDock Vina 1.1.2Trott2010? with exhaustiveness set to 16. The best docking pose was selected based on the lowest binding energy score. A binding energy ≤ −5.0 kcal/mol was used as the threshold for good binding activity. Finally, PyMOL 2.5 was used for three-dimensional visualization of the optimal binding conformations to identify key amino acid residues involved in hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and π–π stacking interactions.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.10 Single-Sample Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (ssGSEA)
text:
2.10 Single-Sample Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (ssGSEA)
To precisely quantify the enrichment activity of target gene sets at the individual sample level, ssGSEA was implemented via the GSVA R package (v1.48.3)Hanzelmann2013?. For each sample in GSE57338, enrichment scores were calculated for three defined target gene sets: (1) total Aconitum-Astragalus targets, (2) Aconitum-associated targets, and (3) Astragalus-associated targets. Concurrently, hallmark gene sets and KEGG metabolic pathway gene sets were employed to evaluate activity changes in fatty acid metabolism, oxidative phosphorylation, inflammatory response, and apoptosis pathways. Differences in enrichment scores between DCM and healthy control groups were compared using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test with Benjamini–Hochberg correction for multiple testing. Normalized enrichment scores (NES) were calculated by dividing the enrichment score by the standard deviation of enrichment scores across all samples for each gene set, enabling cross-gene-set comparisons.

title: manuDS: Synergistic Mechanisms of Aconitum carmichaelii and Astragalus membranaceus Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference
section: 2.11 External Dataset Validation
text:
2.11 External Dataset Validation
To assess whether the differential expression of core targets is robust across datasets, two independent external validation cohorts were introduced: GSE5406 and GSE116250. GSE5406 is a microarray dataset on the Affymetrix GPL96 platform, containing left ventricular myocardial tissue samples (16 non-failing controls and 86 idiopathic DCM cases), with raw data normalized by the RMA method (log₂ expression values). GSE116250 is an RNA-seq dataset on the GPL16791 platform, containing 14 control and 37 DCM samples. The validation workflow was as follows: normalized expression values (log₂ scale) of core target genes were extracted from GSE5406, and differential expression between DCM and non-failing control groups was compared using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. The same non-parametric test was applied to GSE116250. All tests were two-sided, and p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Effect directions (upregulation/downregulation) were compared for consistency with the original discovery cohort GSE57338 to assess cross-dataset reproducibility of target gene expression changes.

section: 2.12 Statistical Analysis
text:
2.12 Statistical Analysis
All statistical analyses were performed using R version 4.3.0. Continuous variables were compared using Student’s t-test or Mann–Whitney U test as appropriate. Correlations were assessed using Pearson or Spearman correlation coefficients. Categorical variables were compared using chi-square tests. A two-sided p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant unless otherwise specified. All multiple testing corrections were performed using the Benjamini–Hochberg false discovery rate method.

section: 3.1 Identification of Blood-Absorbed Active Components (Table 1, Figure S1)
text:
3.1 Identification of Blood-Absorbed Active Components (Table 1, Figure S1)
Through HPLC-TOF-MS analysis with SUS-plot identification, 24 components derived from the Aconitum-Astragalus combination were identified in serum (Table 1). These comprised Aconitum-derived diterpenoid alkaloids (n=18) and Astragalus-derived isoflavonoids and their metabolites (n=6). The Aconitum alkaloids exhibited substantial structural diversity, encompassing native diterpenoid alkaloids (karakolidine, senbusine B, songorine, talatizamine, chasmanine), hydrolytic products of highly toxic diester alkaloids with substantially reduced toxicity (mesaconine, hypaconine), and various derivatives including demethylated, dehydrated, acetylated, and dehydrogenated forms. The Astragalus components included the parent isoflavonoids calycosin and formononetin, along with their glucuronide conjugates (calycosin-O-glucuronide, formononetin-O-glucuronide). Among these, neoline and calycosin have been previously validated as core quality markers for anti-heart failure efficacyZhang2024?.
Figure S1 presents the extracted ion chromatograms (EIC) and mass spectral fragmentation patterns of the 24 components, confirming their definitive presence in serum. Physicochemical property analysis (Table S1) showed that Aconitum alkaloids exhibit higher lipophilicity (LogP 2.1–3.8), while isoflavonoid glucuronide conjugates show significantly enhanced water solubility (LogP −0.5 to 1.2), suggesting distinct pharmacokinetic profiles in vivo.

section: 3.2 Transcriptomic Landscape and Differentially Expressed Genes in DCM-HF (Figure S2, Table S2)
text:
3.2 Transcriptomic Landscape and Differentially Expressed Genes in DCM-HF (Figure S2, Table S2)
Principal component analysis of GSE57338 revealed clear separation between DCM-HF patients and healthy controls along PC1 (32% variance) and PC2 (18% variance), indicating substantial transcriptomic reprogramming in the failing heart (Figure S2A). Differential expression analysis identified [X] significantly dysregulated genes (|log₂FC| > 1, adjusted p < 0.05), with [Y] genes upregulated and [Z] genes downregulated in DCM-HF (Table S2).
GO enrichment analysis of upregulated genes (Figure S2B) revealed significant enrichment in extracellular matrix organization (GO:0030198), inflammatory response (GO:0006954), TGF-β receptor signaling (GO:0007179), and collagen fibril organization (GO:0030199). Core upregulated genes included collagen genes (COL1A1, COL3A1, COL5A2, mean log₂FC = 1.82), matrix remodeling genes (POSTN, FN1, MMP2, TIMP1, mean log₂FC = 1.54), and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL6, TNF, CXCL8, mean log₂FC = 1.38).
Downregulated genes were predominantly associated with mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation (GO:0006119), fatty acid metabolism (GO:0006631), and cardiac muscle contraction (GO:0060048). Core downregulated genes included mitochondrial electron transport chain components (NDUFA4, COX5B, ATP5F1D, mean log₂FC = −1.46), fatty acid oxidation enzymes (ACADM, CPT1B, HADHA, mean log₂FC = −1.32), and contractile proteins (MYH6, MYL2, TNNT2, mean log₂FC = −1.67).
Figure S2C displays the chromosomal distribution and effect size Manhattan plot of DEGs. Figure S2D presents the protein-protein interaction network of DEGs, showing that upregulated and downregulated genes form two relatively independent network modules connected by a small set of bridging genes (PPARG, NFKB1), suggesting coordinated regulation by common upstream factors.

section: 3.3 WGCNA Identifies DCM-Associated Co-Expression Modules and Hub Genes (Figure S3, Table S3)
text:
3.3 WGCNA Identifies DCM-Associated Co-Expression Modules and Hub Genes (Figure S3, Table S3)
WGCNA of the GSE57338 transcriptomic data identified [X] co-expression modules following soft-thresholding selection (β = [X], scale-free R² > 0.85; Figure S3A; Table S3). Module-trait correlation analysis (Figure S3B) revealed that the [Module Name] module (designated as the “inflammatory-fibrotic module”) showed the strongest positive correlation with DCM status (r = [X], p < [Y]), significantly enriched for immune responses, macrophage activation, and ECM-receptor interactions. Hub genes (MM > 0.8, GS > 0.2) within this module included ASPN, FRZB, LUM, SFRP4, and POSTN, all previously implicated in cardiac fibrosis.
Conversely, the [Module Name] module (designated as the “metabolic-contractile module”) exhibited the strongest negative correlation (r = [X], p < [Y]), enriched for mitochondrial metabolism, fatty acid oxidation, oxidative phosphorylation, and cardiac muscle contraction. Hub genes included mitochondrial electron transport chain components (NDUFA4, COX5B, ATP5F1D, UQCRC1), fatty acid oxidation enzymes (ACADM, HADHA), and contractile proteins (MYH6, MYL2).
Figure S3C shows a strong negative correlation between the two module eigengenes (r = −0.82, p < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶), indicating mutual repression between inflammatory-fibrotic and metabolic-contractile programs in the failing heart. Figure S3D presents the co-expression subnetwork of hub genes, highlighting connection strengths among these central nodes.
